passage.text:
IELTS Reading Practice
History & Archaeology
10 Reading Passages  ·  500–600 words each  ·  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)

Contents
Passage 1: Ancient Egypt — The Land of the Pharaohs
Passage 2: The Roman Empire — How Rome Ruled the World
Passage 3: The Discovery of Tutankhamun's Tomb
Passage 4: The Ancient Greeks — Democracy, Philosophy, and Science
Passage 5: Pompeii — The City Frozen in Time
Passage 6: The Silk Road — Ancient Trade Routes That Connected the World
Passage 7: Marie Curie — Pioneer of Science
Passage 8: The Maya Civilisation — An Advanced Ancient Culture
Passage 9: Stonehenge — Britain's Greatest Ancient Mystery
Passage 10: Nelson Mandela — A Life Devoted to Justice

Passage 1: Ancient Egypt — The Land of the Pharaohs
Topic: Ancient Civilisations  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Ancient Egypt is one of the most remarkable civilisations in human history. It developed along the banks of the River Nile in northeastern Africa and lasted for around 3,000 years — from about 3100 BC to 30 BC. The Nile was the heart of Egyptian civilisation. Every year, the river flooded its banks and deposited rich, fertile soil on the surrounding land, allowing farmers to grow large amounts of food in an otherwise very dry region. This reliable food supply supported a large population and allowed society to become increasingly complex. Egypt was ruled by powerful leaders called pharaohs, who were considered both kings and gods. They controlled the government, the army, and the religious life of the country.
Paragraph B
The ancient Egyptians are perhaps best known for their incredible architecture — particularly the pyramids. The pyramids were enormous stone tombs built for the pharaohs. The most famous are the three great pyramids at Giza, near the modern city of Cairo. The largest, the Great Pyramid of Giza, was built for the Pharaoh Khufu around 2560 BC. It stands about 138 metres tall and was made from around 2.3 million stone blocks, each weighing an average of 2.5 tonnes. For over 3,800 years, it was the tallest man-made structure in the world. How the ancient Egyptians managed to build such enormous structures without modern machines remains a subject of great fascination and debate among historians and archaeologists.
Paragraph C
The ancient Egyptians also developed one of the earliest writing systems in the world. They used a system of picture symbols called hieroglyphics, which could represent objects, sounds, or ideas. Hieroglyphics were used for religious texts, records of important events, and royal inscriptions carved into temple walls. For many centuries after the fall of ancient Egypt, nobody could read hieroglyphics — the knowledge of how to decode them had been completely lost. This changed in 1799, when French soldiers discovered a large stone near the Egyptian town of Rosetta. This stone — known as the Rosetta Stone — had the same text written in three different scripts, including hieroglyphics and ancient Greek. By comparing the two known languages with the unknown hieroglyphics, scholars were eventually able to decode the ancient writing system.
Paragraph D
Egyptian civilisation made many other important contributions to the world. The Egyptians developed one of the earliest calendars, based on the movements of the sun, with 365 days in a year — very similar to the calendar we use today. They made significant advances in medicine — Egyptian medical texts describe treatments for wounds, broken bones, and various illnesses, and some of their methods were surprisingly effective. They also made important contributions to mathematics and engineering. The ancient Egyptians' belief in life after death drove them to preserve the bodies of the dead through a process called mummification, and to fill tombs with food, clothing, and other objects that the dead person might need in the afterlife. These tombs have provided archaeologists with an extraordinary amount of information about how ancient Egyptians lived.

Passage 2: The Roman Empire — How Rome Ruled the World
Topic: Ancient Civilisations  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
The Roman Empire was one of the greatest and most influential civilisations in history. At its height, around 117 AD, the Roman Empire stretched from Britain in the northwest to Mesopotamia (modern Iraq) in the east, and from the Rhine and Danube rivers in the north to North Africa in the south. It covered an area of about 5 million square kilometres and had a population of around 70 million people. The Romans were exceptional builders, administrators, and soldiers. Their military was one of the most powerful and well-organised forces the ancient world had ever seen. The Roman army conquered new lands, maintained peace across the empire, and built roads, forts, and walls to defend the borders.
Paragraph B
Rome began as a small city on the banks of the River Tiber in Italy. According to legend, it was founded in 753 BC by twin brothers Romulus and Remus. For several centuries it was a republic, governed by elected officials and a senate — a council of leading citizens. As Rome grew more powerful and its territory expanded, the republican system became harder to manage. After a period of civil wars, the Roman general Julius Caesar took control of Rome in the mid-first century BC. After his assassination in 44 BC, his adopted son Augustus became the first Roman emperor in 27 BC. The period of rule by emperors — known as the Roman Empire — began at that point and lasted for around 500 years in the west.
Paragraph C
The Romans left a lasting impact on the world that can still be felt today. The Latin language they spoke is the direct ancestor of modern languages including Italian, Spanish, French, Portuguese, and Romanian. Many English words also have Latin roots. Roman law formed the basis of the legal systems of many European and Latin American countries. The Romans built an extensive network of roads across their empire — many of these roads still exist today as the foundations of modern roads and motorways. Roman architecture — including arches, domes, and aqueducts — influenced building styles for centuries. The aqueducts they built to carry fresh water to cities were impressive feats of engineering, and some continue to function to this day.
Paragraph D
The western Roman Empire eventually fell in 476 AD, when a Germanic leader named Odoacer overthrew the last Roman emperor, Romulus Augustulus. Historians have debated for centuries why the empire collapsed. The most common explanations include economic problems, military pressure from outside forces, political instability, the spread of disease, and the empire simply becoming too large to govern effectively. However, the eastern half of the Roman Empire — known as the Byzantine Empire — survived for almost another 1,000 years, eventually falling in 1453 when the Ottoman Turks captured its capital, Constantinople. The influence of Rome on Western culture, language, and law has been so profound that it is hard to imagine what Europe and the world might look like without it.

Passage 3: The Discovery of Tutankhamun's Tomb
Topic: Archaeological Discoveries  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
On 4 November 1922, a young water boy working for a British archaeologist made one of the most extraordinary discoveries in the history of archaeology. While digging in the Valley of the Kings — a rocky area on the west bank of the Nile in Egypt, where ancient pharaohs were buried — the boy uncovered a step carved into the rock. This step led to a long staircase and then a sealed door. The archaeologist, Howard Carter, was immediately called to the site. Carter had been searching for the tomb of the young pharaoh Tutankhamun — known today as "King Tut" — for five long years, and he was beginning to doubt that it even existed. The discovery of that step changed everything.
Paragraph B
When the sealed door was opened, Carter and his team found themselves looking into a treasure-filled room. Carter later described the moment he peered through a small hole into the chamber: "At first I could see nothing... then, as my eyes grew accustomed to the light, details of the room within emerged slowly from the mist, strange animals, statues, and gold — everywhere the glint of gold." The tomb contained over 5,000 objects, including golden thrones, painted chariots, model boats, clothing, jewellery, and the famous golden death mask of Tutankhamun. It was the most complete ancient Egyptian royal tomb ever discovered. Most royal tombs had been robbed by thieves thousands of years earlier, but Tutankhamun's tomb had remained almost completely undisturbed for over 3,200 years.
Paragraph C
Tutankhamun himself is a fascinating historical figure. He became pharaoh at around the age of nine, following the death of the previous ruler — believed by many scholars to be his father — the controversial pharaoh Akhenaten, who had attempted to change Egyptian religion by making the sun god Aten the only deity. Tutankhamun reversed these religious changes when he came to power. He died at around the age of eighteen or nineteen, and the cause of his death has been debated by historians and medical experts for many years. Studies of his mummified remains have suggested he suffered from several health problems and may have died from a combination of a broken leg and malaria, though this remains uncertain.
Paragraph D
The discovery of Tutankhamun's tomb attracted worldwide media attention and sparked a global fascination with ancient Egypt. Howard Carter spent the next ten years carefully cataloguing and removing the thousands of objects from the tomb. The golden death mask, which covered the pharaoh's mummified face and is made of solid gold, became one of the most iconic objects in the world and is now on display at the Egyptian Museum in Cairo. The discovery also gave rise to the popular legend of the "Curse of the Pharaohs" — a supposed ancient curse that would bring bad luck or death to anyone who disturbed the tomb. Although several people connected with the discovery did die in the years that followed, most historians and scientists regard the "curse" as a myth rather than fact.

Passage 4: The Ancient Greeks — Democracy, Philosophy, and Science
Topic: Ancient Civilisations  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Ancient Greece is often called the birthplace of Western civilisation. From around 800 BC to 146 BC, the ancient Greeks made an enormous contribution to the ideas, arts, science, and political systems that would shape the modern world. Ancient Greece was not a single country but a collection of independent city-states — such as Athens, Sparta, Corinth, and Thebes — each with its own government, laws, and customs. Athens was the most culturally and intellectually important of these city-states. It was in Athens that democracy was first developed — a system of government in which citizens vote on important decisions. This was a completely new idea at the time, when most societies were ruled by kings or emperors without any input from ordinary people.
Paragraph B
The ancient Greeks produced some of the most important thinkers in history. Philosophy — the study of fundamental questions about life, knowledge, and existence — was developed in ancient Greece. Socrates, who lived in Athens in the fifth century BC, is considered the father of Western philosophy. He developed a method of asking probing questions to help people think more clearly about important topics — a technique still used in education today, known as the "Socratic method." His student Plato wrote down many of his ideas and developed his own theories about the ideal society and the nature of knowledge. Plato's student Aristotle studied and wrote about almost every subject — from biology and astronomy to politics and drama — and his work influenced scholars for over 2,000 years.
Paragraph C
The ancient Greeks also made significant advances in mathematics and science. Pythagoras developed mathematical theorems that are still taught in schools around the world today. Archimedes made important discoveries in mathematics, physics, and engineering — including the famous story that he discovered the principle of water displacement while stepping into a bath. Hippocrates, known as the "father of medicine," taught that diseases had natural causes rather than being sent by the gods, and developed an ethical code for doctors — the Hippocratic Oath — which is still used in modified form by doctors today. Euclid laid the foundations of geometry, and his work "Elements" remained the main textbook for teaching mathematics for over 2,000 years.
Paragraph D
The cultural achievements of ancient Greece were equally impressive. Greek theatre — including tragedy and comedy — was performed in large, open-air theatres for audiences of thousands. Great playwrights such as Sophocles, Euripides, and Aristophanes wrote works that are still performed today. The ancient Greeks built beautiful temples decorated with sculptures, including the famous Parthenon on the Acropolis hill in Athens, which still stands today as one of the finest examples of ancient architecture. The Olympic Games — a festival of athletic competition — were first held in ancient Greece in 776 BC. Although the original games were stopped in 393 AD, they were revived in 1896 and are now held every four years as one of the world's most celebrated sporting events.

Passage 5: Pompeii — The City Frozen in Time
Topic: Archaeological Discoveries  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
On the morning of 24 August, 79 AD, the inhabitants of the Roman city of Pompeii were going about their normal daily lives — shopping in the markets, working in bakeries, and relaxing in the public baths. Then, without much warning, the nearby volcano Mount Vesuvius erupted with tremendous force. A massive cloud of ash, pumice, and hot gases rose several kilometres into the sky. Over the following hours and days, the city of Pompeii — located about 8 kilometres from the volcano — was buried under a layer of volcanic ash and rock nearly 4 to 6 metres deep. Approximately 2,000 of the city's estimated 11,000 inhabitants died in the eruption, either from the heat and toxic gases or from being buried.
Paragraph B
For almost 1,700 years, Pompeii lay buried and forgotten under the hardened volcanic material. Then, in 1748, a group of explorers began excavating the site and discovered that the city had been preserved in remarkable detail beneath the ash. The volcanic material had acted like a protective shell, keeping the buildings, streets, household objects, and even the remains of people and animals in an extraordinary state of preservation. Excavations revealed intact houses with painted walls, shops with food still on the shelves, public buildings, gardens, and street signs. The site gave archaeologists an incredible window into everyday life in the Roman world — information that would have been impossible to obtain from written historical records alone.
Paragraph C
One of the most haunting aspects of Pompeii is the casts of the victims. When the bodies of the dead decomposed inside the hardened ash, they left hollow spaces in exactly the shape of the body. In the 19th century, archaeologists discovered that by pouring plaster into these spaces, they could create detailed casts that show exactly how the people of Pompeii looked in their final moments. Some are curled up with their faces in their hands. Others appear to be clutching each other. A dog, still attached to a chain, was also preserved in this way. These casts give a deeply moving and direct human connection to an event that happened nearly 2,000 years ago.
Paragraph D
Pompeii has taught archaeologists an enormous amount about Roman daily life. Researchers have studied the layout of the city's streets, the design of homes for rich and poor residents, the range of food available, trade connections with other parts of the Roman Empire, and even the graffiti written on the walls. Some of the graffiti is surprisingly modern in tone — people writing about love, politics, and local gossip. Today, Pompeii is one of the most visited archaeological sites in the world and is protected as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. However, the site faces ongoing challenges — the ruins are fragile and are damaged by rain, tourism, and the passage of time, requiring constant and careful conservation work.

Passage 6: The Silk Road — Ancient Trade Routes That Connected the World
Topic: History & Trade  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
The Silk Road was not a single road but a vast network of trade routes that connected China and East Asia with Central Asia, the Middle East, Europe, and North Africa. It was used for trade for over 1,500 years — roughly from 130 BC to the mid-15th century AD. The name "Silk Road" was given to these routes by a German geographer in the 19th century, because silk was one of the most valuable goods traded along it. China had a monopoly on silk production for many centuries, and Chinese silk was enormously prized in Europe and the Middle East. Along with silk, merchants traded many other goods — including spices, precious stones, glassware, cotton, ivory, and gold.
Paragraph B
The Silk Road was incredibly important not just for the movement of goods but also for the exchange of ideas, technologies, and cultures. Buddhism spread from India to China and the rest of East Asia along the Silk Road. Islam spread westward and eastward through trade and the movement of people along these routes. Paper-making, printing, and gunpowder — all invented in China — made their way to Europe via the Silk Road, where they eventually helped drive the Renaissance and other great changes in society. Artistic styles, musical instruments, and agricultural techniques also spread along the routes. The Silk Road was essentially the first great global network of cultural and economic exchange.
Paragraph C
Trading along the Silk Road was an extremely difficult and dangerous journey. The routes passed through some of the world's most challenging environments — including vast deserts, high mountain passes, and extreme climates. No single merchant typically made the entire journey — instead, goods passed through the hands of many different traders as they moved from region to region. Camel caravans were the most common form of transport across the desert sections, as camels could carry heavy loads and survive long periods without water. The journey from China to the Mediterranean took many months and sometimes years. Despite the difficulties and dangers, the potential for great profit kept trade flowing along these routes for many centuries.
Paragraph D
The Silk Road began to decline in the 15th century, mainly because European sailors discovered new sea routes connecting Europe directly with Asia. These sea routes were cheaper, faster, and safer than the overland Silk Road, and they quickly became the preferred method of trade between East and West. The fall of Constantinople — the key city connecting Europe and Asia — to the Ottoman Turks in 1453 also disrupted trade along the land routes. In more recent times, there has been renewed interest in reviving these ancient connections. China's modern "Belt and Road Initiative" — a massive programme of infrastructure investment in roads, railways, and ports connecting Asia, Africa, and Europe — is sometimes described as a modern version of the Silk Road.

Passage 7: Marie Curie — Pioneer of Science
Topic: Historical Figures  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Marie Curie is one of the most remarkable scientists in history. Born Maria Sklodowska in Warsaw, Poland, in 1867, she grew up in a country that did not allow women to attend university. Determined to receive a proper scientific education, she made an agreement with her sister — each would support the other financially while one studied, then switch. Marie moved to Paris in 1891 and enrolled at the Sorbonne university. Despite being one of the very few women in the science faculty and having very little money, she worked incredibly hard and graduated first in her physics degree and second in mathematics. She later married French physicist Pierre Curie, who became both her husband and close scientific partner.
Paragraph B
Together, Marie and Pierre Curie made groundbreaking discoveries in the field of radioactivity — the release of energy from unstable atoms. Building on the earlier work of physicist Henri Becquerel, who discovered that uranium produced mysterious rays, Marie investigated the source of these rays and found that they came from within the atoms of uranium itself — not from any chemical reaction. She coined the word "radioactivity" to describe this phenomenon. She then discovered two previously unknown chemical elements — polonium, which she named after her home country of Poland, and radium. These discoveries transformed our understanding of matter and eventually led to many important applications in medicine, including the treatment of cancer.
Paragraph C
Marie Curie's achievements were recognised with the highest scientific honours. In 1903, she became the first woman to receive a Nobel Prize — the world's most prestigious scientific award — when she shared the Nobel Prize in Physics with Pierre Curie and Henri Becquerel for their research into radioactivity. Remarkably, in 1911 she was awarded a second Nobel Prize — this time in Chemistry — for her discovery of radium and polonium. She remains the only person in history to have won Nobel Prizes in two different scientific fields. She was also the first woman to become a professor at the Sorbonne university in Paris, and she used her fame and status to advocate for the role of women in science.
Paragraph D
Marie Curie's life was not without tragedy. Her husband Pierre was killed in a street accident in 1906, leaving her devastated. She continued her research alone and coped with her grief partly by throwing herself into her work. She developed mobile X-ray units — nicknamed "Little Curies" — which were used during the First World War to help doctors examine wounded soldiers without performing unnecessary surgery. She died in 1934 from a blood disease almost certainly caused by her years of exposure to radioactive materials — at a time when the dangers of radiation were not fully understood. Marie Curie's courage, dedication, and brilliance continue to inspire scientists and women around the world today.

Passage 8: The Maya Civilisation — An Advanced Ancient Culture
Topic: Ancient Civilisations  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
The Maya were one of the most advanced civilisations of the ancient Americas. They flourished in what is now southern Mexico, Guatemala, Belize, Honduras, and El Salvador for over 3,000 years. At the height of their civilisation — during what historians call the Classic period, from about 250 AD to 900 AD — the Maya built enormous cities with impressive stone temples, palaces, and plazas. Their largest cities, such as Tikal in Guatemala and Chichen Itza in Mexico, had populations of tens of thousands of people. The Maya were skilled farmers who grew maize, beans, and squash, and they developed complex systems of irrigation and terraced farming to grow food in the challenging tropical landscape.
Paragraph B
The Maya made remarkable achievements in science and mathematics. They developed an accurate calendar system — in fact, they had several different calendars for different purposes. Their calendar was based on careful astronomical observations, tracking the movements of the sun, moon, and planets. The Maya were among the first civilisations in the world to use the concept of zero in mathematics, which was a revolutionary idea that made complex calculations possible. Their writing system — a sophisticated system using symbols called glyphs — was one of the most developed writing systems in the ancient Americas. Using this system, the Maya recorded their history, religious knowledge, and astronomical observations in books called codices, made from tree bark.
Paragraph C
The Maya built impressive stone temples and pyramids that still stand today. Their temples were used for religious ceremonies and as observatories for watching the movements of the stars and planets. Many Maya temples are aligned with the sun's position at specific times of year — such as the spring and autumn equinoxes, when day and night are equal length. At Chichen Itza, during the spring equinox, shadows fall on the pyramid called El Castillo in a way that creates the illusion of a giant serpent moving down the staircase — an astonishing feat of architectural planning. The Maya also produced beautiful works of art — including carved stone monuments called stelae, painted ceramic pots, and intricate jade jewellery.
Paragraph D
The Maya civilisation experienced a significant collapse in many of its major cities during the 9th century AD. Within a relatively short period, many large Maya cities in the lowland areas were abandoned. Historians have proposed several explanations, including prolonged drought that destroyed farming, overpopulation, warfare between city-states, and political instability. However, the Maya did not entirely disappear. Many Maya people and communities survived in other regions, and their descendants still live in Mexico and Central America today — millions of people speak Maya languages as their first language. The study of the Maya continues to produce new discoveries, particularly as archaeologists use modern technology like satellite imaging to discover previously unknown cities hidden under the jungle.

Passage 9: Stonehenge — Britain's Greatest Ancient Mystery
Topic: Archaeological Discoveries  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Stonehenge is one of the most famous prehistoric monuments in the world. It stands on Salisbury Plain in the county of Wiltshire in southern England and consists of a large circular arrangement of enormous standing stones. The site has been built and modified over a very long period — the earliest parts date back to around 3000 BC, while the large standing stones were erected between about 2500 and 2000 BC. This means that Stonehenge is around 4,000 to 5,000 years old — built before the invention of the wheel was in widespread use in Britain and long before any written records were kept. Despite its age, it has survived remarkably well and remains one of Europe's most visited heritage sites.
Paragraph B
The stones at Stonehenge are of two main types. The larger stones — called sarsens — weigh up to 25 tonnes each. They were transported from a quarry at Marlborough Downs, about 25 kilometres away. The smaller stones — called bluestones — weigh between 2 and 5 tonnes. What makes these remarkable is that they were brought from the Preseli Hills in Wales — around 250 kilometres away. How prehistoric people without modern machinery managed to move these enormous stones over such long distances is still debated by researchers. Suggestions include using wooden rollers and sledges, floating the stones along rivers and the coast on rafts, or even dragging them across snow and ice.
Paragraph C
The purpose of Stonehenge has been a subject of debate and speculation for centuries. One well-supported theory is that it was used as a ceremonial or religious site. Many ancient burial mounds — where people were buried with objects for the afterlife — surround the monument, suggesting it was an important site for funeral ceremonies. Another theory focuses on astronomy. The stones are carefully aligned with the position of the sun at the summer solstice — the longest day of the year — and the winter solstice — the shortest day. During the summer solstice, the sun rises precisely over a stone called the Heel Stone and shines directly into the centre of the monument. This suggests that Stonehenge was used as a kind of astronomical calendar to mark the changing seasons.
Paragraph D
Recent archaeological research has provided new insights into the people who built Stonehenge. Scientists have analysed human remains found near the site and discovered that some of the people buried there came from as far away as Wales and the Mediterranean — suggesting that Stonehenge attracted visitors and pilgrims from across Britain and beyond. Analysis of animal bones found nearby shows that large feasts were held at the site, involving animals brought from different parts of Britain. This evidence suggests that Stonehenge may have functioned not only as a religious site but also as a place where different communities came together. Today, Stonehenge is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and attracts around a million visitors every year, many of whom gather to watch the sun rise over the Heel Stone on the summer solstice.

Passage 10: Nelson Mandela — A Life Devoted to Justice
Topic: Historical Figures  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Nelson Mandela is one of the most admired political leaders of the twentieth century. He was born on 18 July 1918 in a small village in the Eastern Cape region of South Africa. His original name was Rolihlahla, but he became known worldwide as Nelson — a name given to him by a teacher. He grew up under the system of apartheid — a deeply unjust political system in South Africa that enforced strict racial separation and gave white South Africans all the power, while denying black South Africans basic rights such as the right to vote, to own land in most areas, or to attend the same schools and use the same facilities as white people.
Paragraph B
Mandela became involved in politics as a young man, joining the African National Congress (ANC) — the main political organisation fighting against apartheid. He trained as a lawyer and initially supported peaceful protest. However, after the Sharpeville Massacre in 1960 — when South African police killed 69 unarmed black protesters — Mandela and other ANC leaders concluded that peaceful methods alone were not enough. He helped establish a military wing of the ANC that carried out sabotage attacks on government buildings and power stations — deliberately avoiding killing people. In 1964, Mandela was arrested and charged with sabotage and conspiracy to overthrow the government. At his trial, he gave a powerful speech ending with the words: "It is an ideal for which I am prepared to die."
Paragraph C
Mandela was sentenced to life imprisonment and was held on Robben Island — a prison off the coast of Cape Town — for 18 of his 27 years in prison. The conditions were harsh — he spent years doing hard labour in a quarry and was kept in a tiny cell. Despite this, he refused to compromise his political beliefs in exchange for early release, even when the government offered to free him on the condition that he renounce violence. During his time in prison, Mandela became a symbol of resistance against apartheid. International pressure on the South African government grew throughout the 1980s, including economic sanctions from many countries and a worldwide cultural boycott. The campaign to "Free Mandela" became one of the most important political movements of the era.
Paragraph D
After 27 years in prison, Nelson Mandela was finally released on 11 February 1990. His release marked the beginning of the end of apartheid. Negotiations between the ANC and the South African government led to the first democratic elections in South Africa in April 1994, in which all citizens — regardless of race — could vote for the first time. Mandela was elected president with over 62% of the vote. He served as president from 1994 to 1999, during which he focused on building a new, united South Africa through policies of reconciliation — encouraging forgiveness rather than revenge between different racial groups. He was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1993. Nelson Mandela died on 5 December 2013 at the age of 95 and is remembered around the world as a symbol of justice, forgiveness, and human dignity.
